meta.focus: Distinguish stem + そうだ (looks like ~) from plain form + そうだ (I heard that ~).

sections.title: Appearance & Hearsay — そうだ
sections.summary: そうだ can mean two completely different things depending on what comes before it: something LOOKS a certain way (appearance) vs you HEARD it is a certain way (hearsay). The difference is all in what attaches before そう.
sections.whyItMatters: Both patterns appear constantly in everyday Japanese — in weather comments, restaurant recommendations, gossip, and news reports. Using the wrong pattern creates a completely wrong meaning: おいしそうです means 'it looks delicious' but おいしいそうです means 'I heard it's delicious'. One small sound changes the entire source of information.
youWillLearn: Appearance そうだ: ます-stem / adjective stem + そうだ (looks like ~, based on own observation)
Hearsay そうだ: plain form + そうだ (I heard that ~, based on reported information)
The critical distinction — おいしそう vs おいしいそうだ
Negative forms for both patterns (and why they diverge)
How to tell them apart at a glance

sections.explanation:
Appearance そうだ expresses that something looks or seems a certain way — based on the speaker's own visual or sensory observation. It attaches directly to the STEM, not the plain form.

Formation rules:
• Verb: use the ます-stem (e.g. ふる → ふり → ふりそうだ)
• い-adjective: drop the final い (e.g. おいしい → おいし → おいしそうだ)
• な-adjective: bare stem only (e.g. 元気 → 元気そうだ)
• IRREGULAR: いい → よさそうだ (not いそうだ — this form does not exist)

Polite form: そうです (e.g. おいしそうです). Plain/casual: そうだ (e.g. おいしそうだ).
notes: Negative of い-adjective: drop い → add くなさそう. Example: おいしい → おいしくなさそう ('doesn't look delicious').
Negative of な-adjective: stem + じゃなさそう. Example: 元気 → 元気じゃなさそう ('doesn't look well').
The irregular いい → よさそう is the ONLY irregular form. All other adjectives follow the drop-い rule.
This is the speaker's own assessment from direct observation — you can see it, hear it, smell it, or feel it directly.

parts.gloss: dark (て-form connector — it got dark and...)
ます-stem of ふる (to fall/rain)

examples.en: The sky is dark, and it looks like it will rain.
examples.breakdown: ふり is the ます-stem of ふる (to fall/rain). Stem + そうです = appearance based on observing the dark sky.

parts.gloss: stem of おいしい (drop い)

examples.breakdown: おいしい → drop い → おいし + そうです. The speaker is looking at the cake right now.
な-adjective: bare stem 元気 + そうです. The speaker can observe Yamakawa-san directly.

sections.title: Appearance そうだ — Formation Chart
sections.description: How to form appearance そうだ from each word type.

rows.label: い-adj negative
cells: drop い + くなさそう
おいしくなさそうです

notes: よさそう is the only irregular form — いい does NOT become いそう.
な-adjective negative: stem + じゃなさそう (e.g. 元気じゃなさそう = doesn't look well).

sections.meaning: I heard that ~ / apparently ~ / they say ~
sections.explanation:
Hearsay そうだ reports information the speaker received from another source — a friend, the news, a rumour. It attaches to the PLAIN FORM of verbs and adjectives, and to the plain copula (だ) for nouns and な-adjectives.

Formation rules:
• Verb: plain/dictionary form + そうだ (e.g. ふる → ふるそうだ)
• い-adjective: full plain form, keep the い (e.g. おいしい → おいしいそうだ)
• な-adjective: stem + だ + そうだ (e.g. 元気 → 元気だそうだ)
• Noun: noun + だ + そうだ (e.g. 雨 → 雨だそうだ)

Key contrast: ふるそうだ (hearsay — dictionary form) vs ふりそうだ (appearance — ます-stem).
notes: For hearsay, negation goes INSIDE the reported clause: おいしくないそうだ ('I heard it's not delicious') — not おいしくなさそうだ (which would mix both patterns).
Polite: そうです (e.g. ふるそうです). Plain/casual: そうだ (e.g. ふるそうだ).
You can add a source phrase before the main clause: 友だちに聞いたんですが、～そうです = 'I heard from a friend that...'
The source can be hearsay, reading, TV, or any second-hand information — you were not there to observe it yourself.

parts.gloss: I heard that ~ (hearsay そうです)

examples.en: I heard it will rain tomorrow.
examples.breakdown: ふる is the dictionary form — NOT the ます-stem ふり. Dictionary form + そうです = hearsay (reported information, not own observation).

parts.gloss: delicious — full plain form, keep the い
I heard that ~ (hearsay そうです)

examples.en: I heard that shop is delicious.
examples.breakdown: おいしい keeps the full い — this is the full plain form. Compare with appearance: おいしそうです (stem, drop い).

parts.gloss: I heard that ~ (hearsay そうです)

examples.en: I heard Ken-san is doing well.
examples.breakdown: な-adjective: bare stem 元気 + plain copula だ + そうです. The speaker was told this, not observed it.

sections.title: Appearance そうだ vs Hearsay そうだ

items.label: Appearance そうだ
points: Attaches to the STEM (ます-stem for verbs; drop い for i-adj; bare stem for na-adj)
Based on the speaker's OWN direct observation
Signal: there is no plain-form marker before そうだ

parts.gloss: looks like (speaker can see dark clouds)

example.en: It looks like it will rain. (I can see the dark clouds.)

items.label: Hearsay そうだ
points: Attaches to the PLAIN FORM (dictionary form for verbs; full adj with い; noun/na-adj + だ)
Based on REPORTED information — heard from someone or somewhere
Meaning: 'I heard that ~ / apparently ~ / they say ~'
Signal: a plain-form verb or adjective (or だ) immediately before そうだ

example.en: I heard it will rain tomorrow. (I saw the forecast.)

sections.tip: The test: おいしそう or おいしいそうだ? Looking at the food right now → stem → おいしそう (drop い). Someone told you → plain form → おいしいそうだ (keep い). One い makes all the difference.

sections.meaning: Negative forms diverge — this is the hardest part
sections.explanation:
Negatives are where students most often make errors, because the two patterns use completely different constructions:

• Appearance negative (い-adj): drop い → add くなさそう
  Example: おいしい → おいしくなさそう ('doesn't look delicious')

• Hearsay negative: the negation is INSIDE the reported clause, before そうだ
  Example: おいしくないそうだ ('I heard it's not delicious')

CRITICAL: Do NOT mix them. おいしくなさそうだ is WRONG — it tries to be both patterns at once.
notes: Memory trick: In hearsay, you are reporting exactly what you heard — including the negative. So ない comes first: おいしくない + そうだ.
Appearance negative for な-adj: stem + じゃなさそう (e.g. 元気じゃなさそう).
Hearsay negative for な-adj: stem + じゃないそうだ (e.g. 元気じゃないそうだ = 'I heard [he] is not well').
Verb negative hearsay: plain negative form + そうだ (e.g. 来ないそうだ = 'I heard [she] won't come').

parts.gloss: adverbial form of おもしろい
appearance negative そうです — 'doesn't look ~'

examples.en: This movie doesn't look interesting. (speaker watching the trailer)
examples.breakdown: Appearance negative: おもしろい → drop い → おもしろく + なさそうです. The speaker is observing directly.

parts.gloss: plain negative — negation INSIDE the clause
hearsay そうです — 'I heard that ~'

examples.en: I heard that restaurant is not delicious. (a friend told me)
examples.breakdown: Hearsay negative: the negation (おいしくない) is inside the reported clause. Plain negative + そうです = hearsay.

sections.title: そうだ in Context — Weather, Food, People, News

examples.context: Appearance そうだ — speaker looking at the sky

parts.gloss: dark — adverbial form of 暗い

examples.en: The sky has gotten dark, and it looks like it will rain.
examples.note: Appearance そうだ — ふり is the ます-stem. The speaker is directly observing the weather change.

examples.context: Hearsay そうだ — information from news or forecast
examples.en: Apparently it will rain tomorrow too.
examples.note: Hearsay そうだ — ふる is the plain/dictionary form. The speaker learned this from a source (forecast, news, friend).

examples.context: Appearance そうだ — speaker looking at a bowl of ramen

parts.gloss: stem of おいしい (drop い)

examples.en: This ramen looks delicious, doesn't it.
examples.note: Appearance そうだ — おいし is the stem (drop い). The speaker is looking at the bowl right now.

examples.context: Hearsay そうだ — someone reports about a colleague

parts.gloss: plain copula form of 好き (な-adj + だ)

examples.en: I heard that Suzuki-san likes her new job.
examples.note: Hearsay そうだ — 好きだ is the な-adjective + plain copula だ. The speaker was told this, not observed it.

sections.title: What Does It Look Like? What Did You Hear?
sections.context: りきぞ and けん are walking outside together and notice the weather changing. They pass a café and share observations and news.

lines.jp: 空が暗くなって、雨になりそうですね。
lines.en: The sky has gotten dark — it looks like it might become rain, doesn't it.

lines.jp: そうですね。友だちに聞いたんですが、明日も雨だそうです。
lines.en: You're right. I heard from a friend that it will apparently rain tomorrow too.

lines.jp: あの店のケーキはおいしそうですね。
lines.en: The cake at that shop looks delicious, doesn't it.

lines.jp: あの店ですか。友だちが、あそこはおいしいそうですよ。
lines.en: That shop? My friend said that place is delicious.

lines.jp: そうですね。人がたくさん入っていますね。
lines.en: You're right. Lots of people are going in.

lines.jp: あのう、私もあのケーキを食べたいです。
lines.en: Um, I also want to eat that cake.

lines.jp: そうですね。後でまた来ませんか。
lines.en: I agree. Shall we come back later?

lines.jp: はい！私はあのケーキを食べたいんです。とてもおいしいそうですから。
lines.en: Yes! I really want to eat that cake. I heard it's very delicious.

sections.title: Appearance or Hearsay?
sections.instructions: Each sentence uses そうだ. Decide: is it appearance そうだ (based on direct observation) or hearsay そうだ (based on what you heard)? Answer TRUE for appearance, FALSE for hearsay.

items.explanation: Appearance そうだ. The base is おいし — the い has been dropped from おいしい, revealing the stem. Stem + そうです = appearance (speaker is observing directly).
Hearsay そうだ. The full plain form おいしい is intact — the い is still there. Full plain adjective + そうです = hearsay (reported information).

items.sentence: 明日、雨がふるそうです。
items.explanation: Hearsay そうだ. ふる is the plain/dictionary form of the verb (to fall/rain). Dictionary form + そうです = hearsay. The speaker heard this from a source, not observed it directly.
Appearance そうだ. ふり is the ます-stem of ふる. Stem + そうです = appearance. The speaker is observing the signs of rain right now (e.g. dark clouds).
Appearance そうだ. よさそう is the irregular appearance form of いい (not いそう — that form doesn't exist). よさそう = 'looks good', based on direct observation.

items.sentence: あの店は楽しいそうです。
items.explanation: Hearsay そうだ. 楽しい is the full plain form — the い is present. Full plain adjective + そうです = hearsay. The speaker was told (or read) that the shop is fun.

sections.title: Practice: そうだ — Appearance & Hearsay
sections.instructions: Choose the best answer for each question.

items.q: Which adjective form goes before そうだ for APPEARANCE ('looks ~')?
choices: Plain form (おいしい)
Stem (おいし)
Plain negative (おいしくない)
て-form (おいしくて)
items.explanation: Appearance そうだ attaches to the STEM — for い-adjectives, drop the final い. おいしい → drop い → おいし → おいしそうです. The plain form おいしい (with the い intact) is used for HEARSAY, not appearance.

items.q: Which sentence means 'I heard it will rain tomorrow'?
choices: 明日、雨がふりそうです。
明日、雨がふるそうです。
明日、雨がふりましたそうです。
明日、雨がふったそうです。
items.answer: 明日、雨がふるそうです。
items.explanation: Hearsay そうだ attaches to the plain/dictionary form. ふる is the dictionary form of the verb. ふりそうです would mean 'looks like it will rain' (appearance — using the ます-stem ふり). ふりましたそうです and ふったそうです are grammatically unnatural for this context.

items.q: How do you say 'This room looks quiet' (appearance — observing the room)?
choices: このへやはしずかそうです。
このへやはしずかだそうです。
このへやはしずかなそうです。
このへやはしずかいそうです。
items.answer: このへやはしずかそうです。
items.explanation: しずか is a な-adjective. For appearance そうだ with な-adjectives, use the bare stem only: しずか + そうです = しずかそうです. しずかだそうです would be HEARSAY (I heard the room is quiet). しずかなそうです and しずかいそうです are both incorrect forms.

items.q: What is the correct APPEARANCE NEGATIVE form of おいしい ('doesn't look delicious')?
choices: おいしくなさそう
おいしくないそうだ
おいしなさそう
おいしくてなさそう
items.explanation: Appearance negative for い-adjectives: drop い → add くなさそう. おいしい → drop い → おいし + くなさそう = おいしくなさそう. おいしくないそうだ is the HEARSAY negative form ('I heard it's not delicious') — completely different. おいしなさそう is incorrect (missing the く connector).

items.q: What is the KEY difference between おいしそうです and おいしいそうです?
choices: おいしそうです is polite; おいしいそうです is casual.
おいしそうです means 'it looks delicious'; おいしいそうです means 'I heard it's delicious'.
They mean the same thing.
おいしいそうです is more formal.
items.answer: おいしそうです means 'it looks delicious'; おいしいそうです means 'I heard it's delicious'.
items.explanation: おいしそうです uses the STEM (drop い → おいし) + そうです = appearance, based on direct observation. おいしいそうです uses the full PLAIN FORM (keep い → おいしい) + そうです = hearsay, based on reported information. One い is the difference between 'looking at the food' and 'being told about the food'.
